**Ōmiwa Shrine** (大神神社, *Ōmiwa-jinja*), also known as **Miwa Shrine** (三輪神社, *Miwa-jinja*; alternately written as *Miwa-myōjin* (三輪明神)), is a [Shinto shrine](/wiki/Shinto_shrine "Shinto shrine") located in [Sakurai](/wiki/Sakurai,_Nara "Sakurai, Nara"), [Nara Prefecture](/wiki/Nara_Prefecture "Nara Prefecture"), [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan").[[1]](#cite_note-1) The shrine is notable because it contains no sacred images or objects, since it is believed to serve [Mount Miwa](/wiki/Mount_Miwa "Mount Miwa"), the mountain on which it stands.[[2]](#cite_note-2) For the same reason, it has a worship hall (拝殿, *[haiden](/wiki/Haiden_(Shinto) "Haiden (Shinto)")*), but no place for the deity to be housed (神殿, *[shinden](/wiki/Honden "Honden")*). In this sense, it is a model of what the first Shinto shrines were like.[[3]](#cite_note-Tamura20-3) Ōmiwa Shrine is one of the oldest extant Shinto shrines in Japan and the site has been sacred ground for some of the [earliest religious practices](/wiki/Ko-Shint%C5%8D "Ko-Shintō") in Japan. Because of this, it has sometimes been named as Japan's first shrine. Ōmiwa Shrine is a tutelary shrine of the Japanese [sake](/wiki/Sake "Sake") brewers.[[4]](#cite_note-bs-4)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Cmiwa_Shrine&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Ōmiwa Shrine's history is closely related to Mount Miwa and the religious practices surrounding the mountain. In the early [Kofun period](/wiki/Kofun_period "Kofun period"), Yamato kings and leaders had shifted their attention to *[kami](/wiki/Kami "Kami")* worship on Mount Miwa, and Ōmiwa Shrine was the major institution for this branch of worship.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The style of Shinto surrounding Miwa became later known as Miwa Shinto and is set apart from previous practices by a more structured theological philosophy.

The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [Heian period](/wiki/Heian_period "Heian period").[[6]](#cite_note-6) In 965, [Emperor Murakami](/wiki/Emperor_Murakami "Emperor Murakami") ordered that Imperial messengers be sent to report important events to the guardian *kami* of Japan. These *heihaku* were initially presented to 16 shrines, including Ōmiwa.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Ōmiwa was designated as the chief Shinto shrine (*[ichinomiya](/wiki/Ichinomiya "Ichinomiya")*) for the former [Yamato Province](/wiki/Yamato_Province "Yamato Province").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

From 1871 through 1946, Ōmiwa was officially designated one of the *[Kanpei-taisha](/wiki/Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_Shrines "Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines")* (官幣大社), meaning that it stood in the first rank among government supported shrines.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Religious significance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Cmiwa_Shrine&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Religious significance")]

Main article: [Mount Miwa](/wiki/Mount_Miwa "Mount Miwa")

The Ōmiwa Shrine is directly linked to Mount Miwa in that the mountain is the shrine's *[shintai](/wiki/Shintai "Shintai")*, or "kami-body", instead of a building housing a "kami-body". This type of mountain worship (*[shintai-zan](/wiki/Shintai-zan "Shintai-zan")*) is found in the earliest forms of [Shinto](/wiki/Shinto "Shinto") and has also been employed at [Suwa Shrine](/wiki/Suwa_Taisha "Suwa Taisha") in [Nagano](/wiki/Nagano_Prefecture "Nagano Prefecture"), and formerly at [Isonokami Shrine](/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine "Isonokami Shrine") in [Nara](/wiki/Nara_Prefecture "Nara Prefecture") and [Munakata Shrine](/wiki/Munakata_Shrine "Munakata Shrine") in [Fukuoka](/wiki/Fukuoka_Prefecture "Fukuoka Prefecture").

According to the chronicle *[Nihon Shoki](/wiki/Nihon_Shoki "Nihon Shoki")*, [Emperor Sujin](/wiki/Emperor_Sujin "Emperor Sujin") appealed to Mount Miwa's *[kami](/wiki/Kami "Kami")* when Japan was crippled by [plague](/wiki/Pandemic "Pandemic"). In response, the *kami* [Ōmononushi](/wiki/%C5%8Cmononushi "Ōmononushi") demanded rituals be performed for him at Mount Miwa. He then demanded that the rites be led by [Ōtataneko](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Ctataneko&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ōtataneko (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%A4%A7%E7%94%B0%E7%94%B0%E6%A0%B9%E5%AD%90%E5%91%BD "ja:大田田根子命")], his half-kami, half-human son born from the union with a woman of the [Miwa clan](/wiki/Miwa_clan "Miwa clan"). [Ōtataneko](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Ctataneko&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ōtataneko (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%A4%A7%E7%94%B0%E7%94%B0%E6%A0%B9%E5%AD%90%E5%91%BD "ja:大田田根子命")] performed the rites to satisfaction, and the plague subsided. A building dedicated to [Ōtataneko](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Ctataneko&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ōtataneko (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%A4%A7%E7%94%B0%E7%94%B0%E6%A0%B9%E5%AD%90%E5%91%BD "ja:大田田根子命")] was later erected in his honor.

A legendary white snake is said to live in around the shrine and is supposedly one of the kami worshiped there. Indeed, snakes and the snake cult figures importantly in the myths surrounding Mount Miwa as well as early Shinto in general.

## Auxiliary shrines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Cmiwa_Shrine&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Auxiliary shrines")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Hibara_Jinja_by_Yastaka.jpg/250px-Hibara_Jinja_by_Yastaka.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hibara_Jinja_by_Yastaka.jpg)

Hibara Shrine, the most important sessha of Ōmiwa Shrine dedicated to young [mitama](/wiki/Mitama "Mitama") (wakamitama) of [Amaterasu](/wiki/Amaterasu "Amaterasu"), [Izanagi](/wiki/Izanagi "Izanagi") and [Izanami](/wiki/Izanami "Izanami"), considered the first origin of [Ise Grand Shrine](/wiki/Ise_Grand_Shrine "Ise Grand Shrine"), called "[Moto-Ise](/w/index.php?title=Motoise_shrines&action=edit&redlink=1 "Motoise shrines (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%85%83%E4%BC%8A%E5%8B%A2 "ja:元伊勢")]".

The Ōmiwa shrine complex includes notable auxiliary shrines (*[setsumatsusha](/wiki/Setsumatsusha "Setsumatsusha")*), including 12 *Sessha* (摂社, *auxiliary shrine*) and 28 *massha* (末社, *branch shrine*) which are marked by small structures falling under Ōmiwa's jurisdiction.[[10]](#cite_note-10) For example, the *sessha* *Ikuhi jinja* enshrines the *kami* who was appointed Ōmiwa's sake brewer in the 4th month of the 8th year of the reign of [Emperor Sujin](/wiki/Emperor_Sujin "Emperor Sujin"). A poem associated with Ikuhi is said to have been composed by [Empress Jingū](/wiki/Empress_Jing%C5%AB "Empress Jingū") on the occasion of a banquet for her son, [Emperor Ōjin](/wiki/Emperor_%C5%8Cjin "Emperor Ōjin"):[[11]](#cite_note-11)

:   :   This is sacred sake

        :   is not my sacred sake.
    :   This sacred sake brewed by Ōmononushi
    :   How long ago
    :   How long ago.

## Hibara Shrine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Cmiwa_Shrine&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Hibara Shrine")]

**Hibara Shrine** is a [subshrine](/w/index.php?title=Subshrine&action=edit&redlink=1 "Subshrine (page does not exist)") of Omiwa Shrine at the foot of [Mount Miwa](/wiki/Mount_Miwa "Mount Miwa") in [Sakurai](/wiki/Sakurai,_Nara "Sakurai, Nara"), [Nara Prefecture](/wiki/Nara_Prefecture "Nara Prefecture"). The shrine is identified as the place where the [Yata-no-Kagami](/wiki/Yata-no-Kagami "Yata-no-Kagami") and the [Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi](/wiki/Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi "Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi") were first enshrined after they were removed from the imperial palace. It is the first of many [Moto-Ise](/w/index.php?title=Motoise_shrines&action=edit&redlink=1 "Motoise shrines (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%85%83%E4%BC%8A%E5%8B%A2 "ja:元伊勢")] shrines.[[12]](#cite_note-greenshinto-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-:0-14) Amaterasu was originally enshrined there before eventually moving to other Moto-Ise shrines and then finally to [Ise Jingu](/wiki/Ise_Jingu "Ise Jingu").[[14]](#cite_note-:0-14) It has an [Iwakura rock](/wiki/Iwakura_rock "Iwakura rock") and a Shinza made of [Sakaki](/wiki/Cleyera_japonica "Cleyera japonica") wood.[[14]](#cite_note-:0-14)

It has a prominent unique closable triple [torii](/wiki/Torii "Torii") gate.[[12]](#cite_note-greenshinto-12)

## Architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Cmiwa_Shrine&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Architecture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Ohmiwa_Shrine_2nd_Torii.jpg/250px-Ohmiwa_Shrine_2nd_Torii.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ohmiwa_Shrine_2nd_Torii.jpg)

Second *torii* leading to the inner sanctuary

Ōmiwa Shrine is situated in a quiet forest and built directly in front of Mount Miwa. An ancient [Japanese cedar](/wiki/Japanese_cedar "Japanese cedar") tree (*Cryptomeria*) can be found on shrine compound and is considered sacred. The shrine has [Mount Miwa](/wiki/Mount_Miwa "Mount Miwa") as its [Shintai](/wiki/Shintai "Shintai"), as a [Kannabi](/wiki/Kannabi "Kannabi") and does not have a [honden](/wiki/Shinto_architecture "Shinto architecture").[[15]](#cite_note-:2-15)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Hibara-jinja%2C_torii_closeup.jpg/250px-Hibara-jinja%2C_torii_closeup.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hibara-jinja,_torii_closeup.jpg)

Miwa Torii demonstrating both their threefold nature and the closed gate

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/AnneauxBorromeensFace.png/120px-AnneauxBorromeensFace.png)](/wiki/File:AnneauxBorromeensFace.png)

Borromean rings motif

Decorations in the form of [Borromean rings](/wiki/Borromean_rings "Borromean rings") are found throughout the shrine's buildings. This ornamentation symbolizes the three rings, as "Miwa" is written with the [kanji](/wiki/Kanji "Kanji") for "three" (三) and "ring" (輪).

Built in 1984, at 32 m the *torii* on its [sandō](/wiki/Sand%C5%8D "Sandō") is the second highest in Japan.[[4]](#cite_note-bs-4) The shrine also has a great *shime torii*, an ancient form of gate made only with two posts and a rope called *[shimenawa](/wiki/Shimenawa "Shimenawa")*. It is one of few shrines that has a "triple-*torii*" (*[miwa torii](/wiki/Miwa_torii "Miwa torii")*) on its grounds. This gate is also one of the few to actually have doors, which bar access to the mountain it enshrines (though [Mount Miwa](/wiki/Mount_Miwa "Mount Miwa") *is* publicly accessible by same-day arrangement with the Sai Jinja office).[[4]](#cite_note-bs-4)

The buildings at Ōmiwa Shrine are a mix of structures built from ancient times to the [Edo period](/wiki/Edo_period "Edo period").

## National treasures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Cmiwa_Shrine&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: National treasures")]

### Important Cultural Assets

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Cmiwa_Shrine&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Important Cultural Assets")]

* The entire shrine compound
* The 17th century *haiden*, or prayer hall, built with cypress [bark](/wiki/Bark_(botany) "Bark (botany)") roofing[[16]](#cite_note-16)
* The "Triple-*torii*" (*miwa torii*)[[17]](#cite_note-17)
* The *shinden* dedicated to Ōtata Neko
* Suit of bronze armor, lacquered red
* A copy of the *[Book of Zhou](/wiki/Book_of_Zhou "Book of Zhou")*, scroll number 19
